teamObjective.explanation: I am opening the first trick, so the primary goal is to lead a card that either wins or lets my partner win while preserving my limited strength for later tricks.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner has 3 unseen cards and, since no cards have been played, is likely to hold a mix of ranks and suits similar to a random hand. There is a reasonable chance they can cover a low spade lead with a higher non-trump or a trump, but I cannot assume any specific card.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent has 3 unseen cards. Given my own hand contains only low non-trump spades, it is probable that at least one opponent holds higher non-trump cards or trumps (rank 7 and red jokers), so a low single lead is unlikely to win outright.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the lowest card (spade 3) minimizes exposure: it is the cheapest possible lead, gives partner the best chance to overtake with a modest card, and keeps my slightly higher spades (4-6) available for later tricks if the opponents take the lead.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is my highest non-trump card; leading it now spends my best card and it still likely loses to any trump or higher non-trump, leaving me with even weaker cards for future tricks.
The 5 is stronger than the 3 but still a low non-trump; playing it sacrifices more strength without meaningfully improving the chance of winning the trick.
The 4 is slightly better than the 3, but the 3 is the minimum-cost lead and preserves all higher cards, so the 3 is preferred.

risks: An opponent may play a trump or higher non-trump and immediately seize the lead.
My partner may be unable to cover the low spade, ceding control to the opposing team.
My remaining cards are all low, so regaining the lead later will be difficult.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the 3 instead of a higher card, I keep my 4-6 for future opportunities and force opponents to spend a real card to take the trick.

notes: No public cards have been played, so all partner and opponent beliefs remain probabilistic priors grounded only in the deal counts and my own hand.
